    19→/-!
    20→# Prime Tuples Conjecture
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [FLC07] Friedlander, J. B. and Luca, F. and Stoiciu, M., On the irrationality of a
    23→divisor function series. Integers (2007).
    24→-/
    25→
    26→open Nat
    27→
    28→namespace PrimeTuplesConjecture
    29→
    30→/-- For any `k ≥ 2`, let `a₁,...,aₖ` and `b₁,...,bₖ` be integers with `aᵢ > 0`. Suppose that for
    31→every prime `p` there exists an integer `n` such that `p ∤ ∏ i, (aᵢ n + bᵢ)`. Then there exist
    32→infinitely many `n` such that `aᵢ n + bᵢ` is prime for all `i`. -/
    33→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    34→theorem prime_tuples_conjecture {k : ℕ} (hk : 2 ≤ k) (a : Fin k → ℕ+) (b : Fin k → ℕ)
    35→    (hab : ∀ p, p.Prime → ∃ n, ¬ p ∣ ∏ i, (a i * n + b i)) :
    36→    Set.Infinite {n | ∀ i : Fin k, (a i * n + b i).Prime} := by sorry
    37→
    38→end PrimeTuplesConjecture
    39→

    32→-/
    33→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    34→theorem twin_primes :
    35→    answer(sorry) ↔ {p : ℕ | Prime p ∧ Prime (p + 2)}.Infinite := by

    25→/-- The set of subset sums of a set `A ⊆ M`. -/
    26→def subsetSums (A : Set M) : Set M :=
    27→  {n | ∃ B : Finset M, ↑B ⊆ A ∧ n = ∑ i ∈ B, i}
    28→
    29→/-- If `A ⊆ B`, then `subsetSums A ⊆ subsetSums B`. -/
    30→@[gcongr]
    31→theorem subsetSums_mono {A B : Set M} (h : A ⊆ B) : subsetSums A ⊆ subsetSums B :=
    32→  fun _ ⟨C, hC⟩ => ⟨C, hC.1.trans h, hC.2⟩
    33→
    34→/-- The set of subset sums of a sequence `ℕ → M`, where repetition is allowed. -/
    35→def subseqSums' (A : ℕ → M) : Set M :=
    36→  {n | ∃ B : Finset ℕ, n = ∑ i ∈ B, A i}
    37→
    38→variable [Preorder M]
    39→
    40→/-- A set `A ⊆ M` is complete if every sufficiently large element of `M` is a subset sum of `A`. -/
    41→def IsAddComplete (A : Set M) : Prop :=
    42→  ∀ᶠ k in Filter.atTop, k ∈ subsetSums A
    43→
    44→/-- If `A ⊆ B` and `A` is complete, then `B` is also complete. -/
    45→@[gcongr]
    46→theorem IsAddComplete.mono {A B : Set M} (h : A ⊆ B) (ha : IsAddComplete A) : IsAddComplete B := by
    47→  filter_upwards [ha] with x hx
    48→  exact (subsetSums_mono h) hx
    49→
    50→/-- A set `A ⊆ M` is complete if every sufficiently large element of `M` is a subset sum of `A`. -/
    51→def IsAddStronglyComplete (A : Set M) : Prop :=
    52→  ∀ ⦃B : Set M⦄, B.Finite → IsAddComplete (A \ B)
    53→
    54→/-- A strongly complete set is complete. -/
    55→theorem IsAddStronglyComplete.isAddComplete {A : Set M} (hA : IsAddStronglyComplete A) :
    56→    IsAddComplete A := by simpa using hA Set.finite_empty
    57→
    58→/-- If `A ⊆ B` and `A` is strongly complete, then `B` is also strongly complete. -/
    59→theorem IsAddStronglyComplete.mono {A B : Set M} (h : A ⊆ B) (ha : IsAddStronglyComplete A) :
    60→    IsAddStronglyComplete B := fun C hC => (ha hC).mono (by grind)
    61→
    62→/-- A sequence `A` is strongly complete if `fun m => A (n + m)` is still complete for all `n`. -/
    63→def IsAddStronglyCompleteNatSeq (A : ℕ → M) : Prop :=
    64→  ∀ n, IsAddComplete (Set.range (fun m => A (n + m)))
    65→
    66→/-- A strongly complete sequence is complete. -/
    67→theorem IsAddStronglyCompleteNatSeq.isAddComplete {A : ℕ → M}
    68→    (hA : IsAddStronglyCompleteNatSeq A) :
    69→    IsAddComplete (Set.range A) := by simpa using hA 0
    70→
    71→open Classical in
    72→/-- If the range of a sequence `A` is strongly complete, then `A` is strongly complete. -/
    73→theorem IsAddStronglyCompleteNatSeq.of_isAddStronglyComplete {A : ℕ → M}
    74→    (h : IsAddStronglyComplete (.range A)) : IsAddStronglyCompleteNatSeq A :=
    75→  fun n => (h (Finset.finite_toSet _)).mono (A := .range A \ ((Finset.range n).image A))
    76→    (fun _ ⟨⟨y, hy⟩, q⟩ => ⟨y - n, by grind⟩)
    77→
    78→/-- If `A` is strongly complete and the preimage of each element is finite, then the range of `A`
    79→is strongly complete. -/
    80→theorem IsAddStronglyCompleteNatSeq.isAddStronglyComplete {A : ℕ → M}
    81→    (h : IsAddStronglyCompleteNatSeq A) (hA : ∀ m, (A ⁻¹' {m}).Finite) :
    82→    IsAddStronglyComplete (.range A) := by
    83→  refine fun B hB => ?_
    84→  obtain ⟨n, hn⟩ := Finset.exists_nat_subset_range (hB.preimage' (fun b _ => hA b)).toFinset
    85→  rw [Set.Finite.toFinset_subset, Finset.coe_range] at hn
    86→  refine (h (n + 1)).mono ?_
    87→  refine fun x ⟨y, hy⟩ => ⟨⟨n + 1 + y, hy⟩, fun hx => ?_⟩
    88→  have : n + 1 + y ∈ Set.Iio n := by grind
    89→  grind
    90→
    91→/-- A sequence `A` is complete if every sufficiently large element of `M` is a sum of
    92→(not necessarily distinct) terms of `A`. -/
    93→def IsAddCompleteNatSeq' (A : ℕ → M) : Prop :=
    94→  ∀ᶠ k in Filter.atTop, k ∈ subseqSums' A
    95→

    21→/-!
    22→# Beurling primes
    23→
    24→*References:*
    25→ - [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beurling_zeta_function)
    26→ - [Beurling Zeta Functions, Generalised Primes, and Fractal Membranes](https://arxiv.org/abs/math/0410270)
    27→-/
    28→
    29→open Filter
    30→
    31→/-- A sequence of real numbers `1 < a 0 < a 1 < ...` is called a set of Beurling prime numbers if
    32→it tends to infinity. -/
    33→noncomputable def IsBeurlingPrimes (a : ℕ → ℝ) : Prop :=
    34→  1 < a 0 ∧ StrictMono a ∧ Tendsto a atTop atTop
    35→
    36→/-- The set of Beurling integers are numbers of the form `∏ i, (a i) ^ (k i)`, where `k` has
    37→finite support. -/
    38→def BeurlingInteger (a : ℕ → ℝ) : Set ℝ :=
    39→  letI g : (ℕ →₀ ℕ) → ℝ := fun k => k.prod (fun x y => (a x) ^ y)
    40→  .range g
    41→
    42→/-- Every element of the sequence `a` is a Beurling integer. -/
    43→lemma generator_mem_beurling (a : ℕ → ℝ) (i : ℕ) : a i ∈ BeurlingInteger a :=
    44→  ⟨Finsupp.single i 1, by aesop⟩
    45→
    46→/-- The set of Beurling integers is closed under multiplication. -/
    47→lemma mul_mem_beurling {a : ℕ → ℝ} {x y : ℝ} (hx : x ∈ BeurlingInteger a) (hy : y ∈ BeurlingInteger a) :
    48→    x * y ∈ BeurlingInteger a := by
    49→  obtain ⟨k, rfl⟩ := hx
    50→  obtain ⟨l, rfl⟩ := hy
    51→  exact ⟨k + l, by simp [Finsupp.prod_add_index', pow_add]⟩
    52→
    53→/-- The set of Beurling integers is closed under taking powers. -/
    54→lemma pow_mem_beurling {a : ℕ → ℝ} {x : ℝ} (k : ℕ) (hx : x ∈ BeurlingInteger a) :
    55→    x ^ k ∈ BeurlingInteger a := by
    56→  induction k with
    57→  | zero => exact ⟨0, by norm_num⟩
    58→  | succ k ih => simpa [pow_succ'] using mul_mem_beurling hx ih
    59→

    16→import Mathlib.NumberTheory.LegendreSymbol.JacobiSymbol
    17→

    19→/-!
    20→# Wall-Sun-Sun primes
    21→
    22→*References:*
    23→- [Wikipedia, Lucas_sequence](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lucas_sequence)
    24→- [Wikipedia, Wall–Sun–Sun prime](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wall%E2%80%93Sun%E2%80%93Sun_prime)
    25→- [Wikipedia, Wieferich prime](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lucas%E2%80%93Wieferich_prime)
    26→-/
    27→
    28→open scoped NumberTheorySymbols
    29→
    30→/--
    31→**The Lucas sequence of the first kind**
    32→$U_0(P, Q) = 0$, $U_1(P, Q)=1$, $U_{n+2}(P, Q)=PU_{n+1}(P, Q)-QU_n(P, Q)$
    33→-/
    34→def LucasSequence.U (P Q : ℤ) : ℕ → ℤ
    35→  | 0 => 0
    36→  | 1 => 1
    37→  | n + 2 => P * LucasSequence.U P Q (n + 1) - Q * LucasSequence.U P Q n
    38→
    39→/--
    40→**The Lucas sequence of the second kind**
    41→$V_0(P, Q) = 0$, $V_1(P, Q)=P$, $V_{n+2}(P, Q)=PV_{n+1}(P, Q)-QV_n(P, Q)$
    42→-/
    43→def LucasSequence.V (P Q : ℤ) : ℕ → ℤ
    44→  | 0 => 2
    45→  | 1 => P
    46→  | n + 2 => P * LucasSequence.V P Q (n + 1) - Q * LucasSequence.V P Q n
    47→
    48→/--
    49→**The Lucas numbers**
    50→$L_0 = 2$, $L_1=1$, $L_{n+2} = L_{n+1}+L_n$
    51→-/
    52→def lucasNumber : ℕ → ℤ := LucasSequence.V 1 (-1)
    53→
    54→/--
    55→**Wall–Sun–Sun prime**
    56→A prime $p$ is a Wall–Sun–Sun prime if and only if $L_p \equiv 1 \pmod{p^2}$, where $L_p$ is
    57→the $p$-th Lucas number.
    58→-/
    59→structure IsWallSunSunPrime (p : ℕ) : Prop where
    60→  prime : p.Prime
    61→  lucasNumber_modeq : lucasNumber p ≡ 1 [ZMOD (p ^ 2)]
    62→
    63→/--
    64→**Lucas–Wieferich prime**
    65→A Lucas–Wieferich prime associated with $(a,b)$ is an odd prime $p$, not dividing $a^2 - 4b$, such
    66→that $U_{p-\varepsilon}(a,b) \equiv 0 \pmod{p^2}$ where $U(a,b)$ is the Lucas sequence of the first
    67→kind and $\varepsilon$ is the Legendre symbol $\left({\tfrac {a^{2}-4b}{p}}\right)$.
    68→
    69→See first paragraph of
    70→https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wall%E2%80%93Sun%E2%80%93Sun_prime#Wall%E2%80%93Sun%E2%80%93Sun_primes_with_discriminant_D
    71→for the condition that $p$ is odd and coprime to the discriminant
    72→-/
    73→structure IsLucasWieferichPrime (a b p : ℕ) : Prop where
    74→  prime : p.Prime
    75→  odd : Odd p
    76→  not_dvd : ¬(p : ℤ) ∣ a ^ 2 - 4 * b
    77→  modeq : LucasSequence.U a b (p - J(a^2 - 4*b | p)).toNat ≡ 0 [ZMOD (p^2)]
    78→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 980
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/980](https://www.erdosproblems.com/980)
    23→
    24→Let $k \geq 2$ and $n_k(p)$ denote the least $k$th power nonresidue of $p$.
    25→Is it true that
    26→$$\sum_{p < x} n_k(p) \sim c_k \cdot \frac{x}{\log x}$$
    27→for some constant $c_k > 0$?
    28→
    29→Proved by Erdős [Er61e] for $k = 2$, with $c_2 = \sum_{j=1}^{\infty} p_j / 2^j$.
    30→The general case was proved by Elliott [El67b].
    31→
    32→[Er65b] Erdős, P.
    33→
    34→[Er61e] Erdős, P.
    35→
    36→[El67b] Elliott, P.D.T.A.
    37→-/
    38→
    39→open Real
    40→
    41→namespace Erdos980
    42→
    43→/-- $a$ is a $k$th power residue modulo $p$ if there exists $b$ with $b^k \equiv a \pmod{p}$. -/
    44→def IsKthPowerResidue (k p a : ℕ) : Prop :=
    45→  ∃ b : ℕ, b ^ k % p = a % p
    46→
    47→/-- The least $k$th power nonresidue of $p$: the smallest positive integer
    48→that is not a $k$th power residue modulo $p$. Returns $0$ if no such number exists. -/
    49→noncomputable def leastKthPowerNonresidue (k p : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    50→  sInf {a : ℕ | 0 < a ∧ ¬IsKthPowerResidue k p a}
    51→
    52→/--
    53→Erdős Problem 980 [Er65b]:
    54→
    55→For every $k \geq 2$, there exists a constant $c_k > 0$ such that
    56→$$\sum_{\substack{p < x \\ p \text{ prime}}} n_k(p) \sim c_k \cdot \frac{x}{\log x}.$$
    57→
    58→Formulated as: there exists $c_k > 0$ such that for every $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists $X_0$
    59→such that for all $x \geq X_0$,
    60→$$\left|\sum_{\substack{p < x \\ p \text{ prime}}} n_k(p) - c_k \cdot \frac{x}{\log x}\right|
    61→\leq \varepsilon \cdot \frac{x}{\log x}.$$
    62→-/
    63→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    64→theorem erdos_980 : answer(True) ↔
    65→    ∀ k : ℕ, k ≥ 2 →
    66→    ∃ c : ℝ, c > 0 ∧
    67→    ∀ ε : ℝ, ε > 0 →
    68→    ∃ X₀ : ℕ, ∀ x : ℕ, x ≥ X₀ →
    69→      |((Finset.filter (fun p => Nat.Prime p) (Finset.range x)).sum
    70→          (fun p => (leastKthPowerNonresidue k p : ℝ))) -
    71→        c * (x : ℝ) / log (x : ℝ)| ≤
    72→      ε * (x : ℝ) / log (x : ℝ) := by
    73→  sorry
    74→
    75→end Erdos980
    76→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 981
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/981](https://www.erdosproblems.com/981)
    23→
    24→For every ε > 0, let f_ε(p) be the smallest m such that ∑_{n≤N} (n/p) < εN for all N ≥ m.
    25→Is it true that ∑_{p<x} f_ε(p) ~ c_ε · x/(log x) for some constant c_ε > 0?
    26→Proved by Elliott [El69].
    27→
    28→[El69] Elliott, P. D. T. A., _On the mean value of $f(p)$_. Proc. London Math. Soc. (3) 21
    29→(1970), 28–96.
    30→-/
    31→
    32→open Filter
    33→
    34→open scoped Topology Real
    35→
    36→namespace Erdos981
    37→
    38→/-- The partial sum of Legendre symbols $(n/p)$ for $n = 1, \ldots, N$.
    39→    We use the Jacobi symbol which coincides with the Legendre symbol for prime $p$. -/
    40→def legendrePartialSum (p : ℕ) (N : ℕ) : ℤ :=
    41→  (Finset.Icc 1 N).sum (fun n => jacobiSym (n : ℤ) p)
    42→
    43→/-- $f_\varepsilon(p)$: the smallest positive integer $m$ such that for all $N \ge m$,
    44→    $\sum_{n \le N} (n/p) < \varepsilon \cdot N$. Returns $0$ if no such $m$ exists. -/
    45→noncomputable def fEpsilon (ε : ℝ) (p : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    46→  sInf {m : ℕ | m ≥ 1 ∧ ∀ N : ℕ, N ≥ m → (legendrePartialSum p N : ℝ) < ε * (N : ℝ)}
    47→
    48→/--
    49→Erdős Problem 981 (proved by Elliott [El69]):
    50→
    51→For every $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists $c_\varepsilon > 0$ such that
    52→$$\sum_{\substack{p < x \\ p \text{ prime}}} f_\varepsilon(p) \sim c_\varepsilon \cdot
    53→\frac{x}{\log x}.$$
    54→
    55→Formulated as: the ratio $\sum_{p < x} f_\varepsilon(p) / (x / \log x)$ tends to
    56→$c_\varepsilon$.
    57→-/
    58→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    59→theorem erdos_981 (ε : ℝ) (hε : ε > 0) :
    60→    ∃ c : ℝ, c > 0 ∧
    61→      Tendsto
    62→        (fun x : ℕ =>
    63→          (((Finset.range x).filter Nat.Prime).sum (fun p => fEpsilon ε p) : ℝ) /
    64→            ((x : ℝ) / Real.log (x : ℝ)))
    65→        atTop (nhds c) := by
    66→  sorry
    67→
    68→end Erdos981
    69→

    20→# Erdős Problem 852
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/852](https://www.erdosproblems.com/852)
    23→
    24→Let $d_n = p_{n+1} - p_n$, where $p_n$ is the $n$-th prime. Let $h(x)$ be maximal
    25→such that for some $n < x$ the numbers $d_n, d_{n+1}, \ldots, d_{n+h(x)-1}$ are
    26→all distinct. Estimate $h(x)$.
    27→
    28→Brun's sieve implies $h(x) \to \infty$ as $x \to \infty$.
    29→
    30→[Er85c] Erdős, P., _Some problems and results on combinatorial number theory_ (1985).
    31→-/
    32→
    33→namespace Erdos852
    34→
    35→/-- The $n$-th prime (0-indexed): $p_0 = 2$, $p_1 = 3$, etc. -/
    36→noncomputable def nthPrime (n : ℕ) : ℕ := Nat.nth Nat.Prime n
    37→
    38→/-- The $n$-th prime gap: $d_n = p_{n+1} - p_n$. -/
    39→noncomputable def primeGap (n : ℕ) : ℕ := nthPrime (n + 1) - nthPrime n
    40→
    41→/-- A run of $k$ consecutive prime gaps starting at position $n$ are all distinct:
    42→$d_n, d_{n+1}, \ldots, d_{n+k-1}$ are pairwise different. -/
    43→def IsDistinctPrimeGapRun (n k : ℕ) : Prop :=
    44→  Function.Injective (fun i : Fin k => primeGap (n + i.val))
    45→
    46→/-- $h(x)$: the maximal length of a run of distinct consecutive prime gaps
    47→$d_n, d_{n+1}, \ldots, d_{n+h-1}$ for some $n < x$. -/
    48→noncomputable def h (x : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    49→  sSup {k : ℕ | ∃ n, n < x ∧ IsDistinctPrimeGapRun n k}
    50→
    51→/--
    52→**Erdős Problem 852** — Lower bound [Er85c]:
    53→
    54→Is it true that there exists a constant $c > 0$ such that for all sufficiently large $x$,
    55→$h(x) > (\log x)^c$?
    56→-/
    57→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    58→theorem erdos_852 : answer(sorry) ↔
    59→    ∃ c : ℝ, c > 0 ∧
    60→    ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ x : ℕ, x ≥ N₀ →
    61→      (h x : ℝ) > (Real.log (x : ℝ)) ^ c := by
    62→  sorry
    63→
    64→/--
    65→**Erdős Problem 852** — Upper bound [Er85c]:
    66→
    67→Is it true that $h(x) = o(\log x)$, i.e., for every $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists $N_0$
    68→such that for all $x \geq N_0$, $h(x) < \varepsilon \cdot \log(x)$?
    69→-/
    70→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    71→theorem erdos_852.variants.upper_bound : answer(sorry) ↔
    72→    ∀ ε : ℝ, ε > 0 →
    73→    ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ x : ℕ, x ≥ N₀ →
    74→      (h x : ℝ) < ε * Real.log (x : ℝ) := by
    75→  sorry
    76→
    77→end Erdos852
    78→

    21→namespace Nat
    22→
    23→/--
    24→A natural number $n$ is said to be $k$-full (or $k$-powerful) if for every prime factor $p$ of $n$,
    25→the $k$-th power $p^k$ also divides $n$.
    26→-/
    27→def Full (k : ℕ) (n : ℕ) : Prop := ∀ p ∈ n.primeFactors, p^k ∣ n
    28→
    29→instance Full.decide : ∀ k n, Decidable (Full k n) := by
    30→  intro k n
    31→  dsimp [Full]
    32→  infer_instance
    33→
    34→/-- Every natural number is $0$-full. -/
    35→theorem Full.zero_left (n : ℕ) : (0 : ℕ).Full n := by
    36→  simp [Nat.Full]
    37→
    38→/-- Every natural number is $1$-full. -/
    39→theorem Full.one_left (n : ℕ) : (1 : ℕ).Full n := by
    40→  aesop (add simp [Nat.Full])
    41→
    42→/-- $0$ is always $k$-full. -/
    43→theorem Full.zero_right (k : ℕ) : k.Full 0 := by
    44→  simp [Nat.Full]
    45→
    46→/-- $1$ is always $k$-full. -/
    47→theorem Full.one_right (k : ℕ) : k.Full 1 := by
    48→  simp [Nat.Full]
    49→
    50→/--
    51→A [Powerful number](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Powerful_number) is a natural number $n$ where
    52→for every prime divisor $p$, $p^2$ divides $n$.
    53→Powerful numbers are also known as "squareful", "square-full", or "$2$-full".
    54→-/
    55→abbrev Powerful : ℕ → Prop := (2).Full
    56→
    57→instance Powerful.decide : ∀ n, Decidable (Powerful n) := by
    58→  intro n
    59→  dsimp [Powerful, Full]
    60→  apply Finset.decidableDforallFinset
    61→
    62→theorem full_of_le_full (k : ℕ) (n : ℕ) {m : ℕ} (hk : k ≤ m) (h : m.Full n) : k.Full n :=
    63→  fun p a ↦ pow_dvd_of_le_of_pow_dvd hk (h p a)
    64→
    65→/-- If $n \equiv p \pmod{p ^ (k + 1)}$, for a prime $p$ then $n$ is not $(k + 1)$-full. -/
    66→theorem not_full_of_prime_mod_prime_sq (n : ℕ) (k : ℕ) {p : ℕ} (hp : p.Prime)
    67→    (h : n % p ^ (k + 1) = p) : ¬ (k + 1).Full n := by
    68→  rw [Full]
    69→  push_neg
    70→  use p
    71→  simp [mem_primeFactors, hp, ne_eq, true_and]
    72→  constructor
    73→  · rw [←Nat.div_add_mod n (p ^ (k + 1)), h]
    74→    have : p ∣ p ^ (k + 1) := by exact Dvd.intro_left (p.pow k) rfl
    75→    simp
    76→    exact ⟨Dvd.dvd.mul_right this (n / p ^ (k + 1)), fun a ↦ Prime.ne_zero hp⟩
    77→  · intro h
    78→    simp_all [Nat.dvd_iff_mod_eq_zero.mp h]
    79→    aesop
    80→
    81→
    82→open Lean Meta Qq in
    83→/-- Simproc to compute the set `Nat.primeFactors`. -/
    84→dsimproc primeFactorsEq (Nat.primeFactors _) := fun e ↦ do
    85→  unless e.isAppOfArity `Nat.primeFactors 1 do return .continue
    86→  let some n ← fromExpr? e.appArg! | return .continue
    87→  let outAsList : List Q(ℕ) := (unsafe n.primeFactors.val.unquot).map mkNatLit
    88→  let outAsFinset : Q(Finset ℕ) := outAsList.foldl (fun s n ↦ q(insert $n $s)) q({})
    89→  return .done outAsFinset

    22→/-- The greatest prime divisor of a natural number `n > 1`.

    25→
    26→TODO Consider redefining this using the computable implementation:
    27→`n.primeFactorsList.getLastI`. -/
    28→noncomputable def maxPrimeFac (n : ℕ) : ℕ := sSup {p : ℕ | p.Prime ∧ p ∣ n}

    31→lemma maxPrimeFac_zero :

    34→
    35→@[simp]
    36→lemma maxPrimeFac_one :

    38→  suffices {p : ℕ | p.Prime ∧ p = 1} = ∅ by simp [maxPrimeFac, this]
    39→  aesop

    41→lemma prime_maxPrimeFac_of_one_lt (n : ℕ) (h : 1 < n) :
    42→    Prime (maxPrimeFac n) := by
    43→  set s := {p : ℕ | p.Prime ∧ p ∣ n} with hs
    44→  have hs₀ : s.Nonempty := by
    45→    simp only [Set.Nonempty, Set.mem_setOf_eq, ← ne_one_iff_exists_prime_dvd, hs]
    46→    omega
    47→  have hs₁ : BddAbove s := by
    48→    use n
    49→    simp only [hs, mem_upperBounds, Set.mem_setOf_eq, and_imp]
    50→    exact fun p _ hp ↦ Nat.le_of_dvd (zero_lt_of_lt h) hp

    54→    (n p : ℕ) (hn : 0 < n) (hp : p.Prime) (h_dvd : p ∣ n) (h_le : maxPrimeFac n ≤ p) :
    55→    maxPrimeFac n = p := by
    56→  refine le_antisymm h_le ?_
    57→  replace hn : 1 < n := Nat.lt_of_lt_of_le hp.one_lt (Nat.le_of_dvd hn h_dvd)
    58→  set s := {p : ℕ | p.Prime ∧ p ∣ n} with hs
    59→  have hs₁ : BddAbove s := by
    60→    use n

    62→    exact fun p _ hp ↦ Nat.le_of_dvd (zero_lt_of_lt hn) hp

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 995
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/995](https://www.erdosproblems.com/995)
    23→
    24→[Er49d] Erdős, P. (1949) — constructed a lacunary sequence and $f \in L^2([0,1])$ such that, for
    25→every $\varepsilon > 0$, for almost all $\alpha$,
    26→$\limsup_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{N(\log \log N)^{1/2 - \varepsilon}} \sum_{k \leq N}
    27→f(\{\alpha n_k\}) = \infty$.
    28→
    29→[Er64b] Erdős, P. (1964) — thought that his lower bound was closer to the truth.
    30→-/
    31→
    32→open MeasureTheory Filter Finset Set
    33→
    34→namespace Erdos995
    35→
    36→/-- A sequence of positive integers is lacunary if it is strictly increasing
    37→and there exists $q > 1$ such that $n_{k+1} \geq q \cdot n_k$ for all $k$. -/
    38→def IsLacunary (n : ℕ → ℕ) : Prop :=
    39→  StrictMono n ∧ ∃ q : ℝ, q > 1 ∧ ∀ k : ℕ, (n k : ℝ) * q ≤ n (k + 1)
    40→
    41→/--
    42→Erdős Problem 995 [Er64b]:
    43→
    44→Is it true that for every lacunary sequence $(n_k)$ of positive integers and every
    45→$f \in L^2([0,1])$, for almost all $\alpha \in [0,1]$,
    46→$$\sum_{k < N} f(\{\alpha \cdot n_k\}) = o(N \cdot \sqrt{\log \log N})?$$
    47→
    48→Formulated as: for every $\varepsilon > 0$, for almost all $\alpha$, eventually (for large $N$),
    49→$\left|\sum_{k < N} f(\{\alpha \cdot n_k\})\right| \leq \varepsilon \cdot N \cdot
    50→\sqrt{\log \log N}$.
    51→-/
    52→@[category research open, AMS 11 42]
    53→theorem erdos_995 : answer(sorry) ↔
    54→    ∀ (n : ℕ → ℕ), IsLacunary n →
    55→    ∀ (f : ℝ → ℝ), Measurable f →
    56→    Integrable (fun x => f x ^ 2) (volume.restrict (Icc (0 : ℝ) 1)) →
    57→    ∀ ε : ℝ, ε > 0 →
    58→    ∀ᵐ α ∂(volume.restrict (Icc (0 : ℝ) 1)),
    59→      ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ N : ℕ, N ≥ N₀ →
    60→        |∑ k ∈ Finset.range N, f (Int.fract (α * (n k : ℝ)))| ≤
    61→          ε * (N : ℝ) * Real.sqrt (Real.log (Real.log (N : ℝ))) := by
    62→  sorry
    63→
    64→end Erdos995

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 992
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/992](https://www.erdosproblems.com/992)
    23→
    24→Let $x_1 < x_2 < \cdots$ be an infinite sequence of integers. Is it true that, for
    25→almost all $\alpha \in [0,1]$, the discrepancy
    26→$$D(N) = \max_{I \subseteq [0,1]} |\#\{n \le N : \{\alpha x_n\} \in I\} - |I| \cdot N|$$
    27→satisfies $D(N) \ll N^{1/2} (\log N)^{o(1)}$? Or even $D(N) \ll N^{1/2} (\log \log N)^{O(1)}$?
    28→
    29→Erdős and Koksma [ErKo49] and Cassels [Ca50] independently proved that, for any
    30→sequence $x_i$ and almost all $\alpha$, $D(N) \ll N^{1/2} (\log N)^{5/2 + o(1)}$. Baker [Ba81]
    31→improved this to $D(N) \ll N^{1/2} (\log N)^{3/2 + o(1)}$.
    32→
    33→This was disproved by Berkes and Philipp [BePh94], who constructed a sequence
    34→of integers $x_1 < x_2 < \cdots$ such that, for almost all $\alpha \in [0,1]$,
    35→$$\limsup_{N \to \infty} D(N) / (N \log N)^{1/2} > 0.$$
    36→-/
    37→
    38→open Filter MeasureTheory Finset
    39→
    40→namespace Erdos992
    41→
    42→/-- The discrepancy of the sequence $\{\alpha \cdot x(n)\} \pmod{1}$ with respect to
    43→subintervals of $[0,1)$.
    44→$$D(N) = \sup_{0 \le a < b \le 1} |\#\{n < N : \{\alpha \cdot x(n)\} \in [a, b)\} - (b - a) \cdot N|$$ -/
    45→noncomputable def discrepancy (x : ℕ → ℤ) (α : ℝ) (N : ℕ) : ℝ :=
    46→  sSup {d : ℝ | ∃ a b : ℝ, 0 ≤ a ∧ a < b ∧ b ≤ 1 ∧
    47→    d = abs (((Finset.range N).filter (fun n =>
    48→      a ≤ Int.fract (α * (x n : ℝ)) ∧ Int.fract (α * (x n : ℝ)) < b)).card -
    49→      (b - a) * (N : ℝ))}
    50→
    51→/--
    52→Erdős Problem 992 (disproved by Berkes and Philipp [BePh94]):
    53→
    54→Is it true that for every strictly increasing sequence of positive integers
    55→$x_1 < x_2 < \cdots$ and almost all $\alpha \in [0,1]$, the discrepancy satisfies
    56→$D(N) \ll N^{1/2} (\log N)^{o(1)}$?
    57→
    58→Here $D(N) \ll N^{1/2} (\log N)^{o(1)}$ is formalized as: for every $\varepsilon > 0$,
    59→there exists $C > 0$ such that $D(N) \le C \sqrt{N} (\log N)^\varepsilon$ for all
    60→sufficiently large $N$.
    61→-/
    62→@[category research solved, AMS 11 28]
    63→theorem erdos_992 : answer(False) ↔
    64→    ∀ (x : ℕ → ℤ), StrictMono x → (∀ n, 0 < x n) →
    65→    ∀ᵐ α ∂(volume.restrict (Set.Icc (0 : ℝ) 1)),
    66→      ∀ ε : ℝ, ε > 0 →
    67→        ∃ C : ℝ, C > 0 ∧
    68→          ∀ᶠ N in atTop,
    69→            discrepancy x α N ≤
    70→              C * Real.sqrt (N : ℝ) * (Real.log (N : ℝ)) ^ ε := by
    71→  sorry
    72→
    73→/--
    74→Erdős Problem 992, stronger variant (also disproved by [BePh94]):
    75→
    76→Is it true that $D(N) \ll N^{1/2} (\log \log N)^{O(1)}$? Here this is formalized as:
    77→there exist $C > 0$ and $K > 0$ such that $D(N) \le C \sqrt{N} (\log \log N)^K$ for
    78→all sufficiently large $N$.
    79→-/
    80→@[category research solved, AMS 11 28]
    81→theorem erdos_992.variants.stronger : answer(False) ↔
    82→    ∀ (x : ℕ → ℤ), StrictMono x → (∀ n, 0 < x n) →
    83→    ∀ᵐ α ∂(volume.restrict (Set.Icc (0 : ℝ) 1)),
    84→      ∃ C : ℝ, C > 0 ∧ ∃ K : ℝ, K > 0 ∧
    85→        ∀ᶠ N in atTop,
    86→          discrepancy x α N ≤
    87→            C * Real.sqrt (N : ℝ) *
    88→              (Real.log (Real.log (N : ℝ))) ^ K := by
    89→  sorry
    90→
    91→/--
    92→Berkes and Philipp [BePh94] disproved Erdős Problem 992 by constructing a
    93→strictly increasing sequence of positive integers such that for almost all
    94→$\alpha \in [0,1]$,
    95→$$\limsup_{N \to \infty} D(N) / (N \log N)^{1/2} > 0.$$
    96→-/
    97→@[category research solved, AMS 11 28]
    98→theorem erdos_992.variants.berkes_philipp :
    99→    ∃ (x : ℕ → ℤ), StrictMono x ∧ (∀ n, 0 < x n) ∧
   100→    ∀ᵐ α ∂(volume.restrict (Set.Icc (0 : ℝ) 1)),
   101→      ∃ c : ℝ, c > 0 ∧
   102→        ∃ᶠ N in atTop,
   103→          discrepancy x α N ≥
   104→            c * Real.sqrt ((N : ℝ) * Real.log (N : ℝ)) := by
   105→  sorry
   106→
   107→end Erdos992

    21→/-!
    22→# Legendre's conjecture
    23→
    24→*References:*
    25→- [Landau Problems Wikipedia Page](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Landau%27s_problems#Twin_prime_conjecture)
    26→- [Legendre Conjecture Wikipedia Page](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Legendre%27s_conjecture)
    27→- [Luan Alberto Ferreira, *Real exponential sums over primes and prime gaps*](https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.08725)
    28→-/
    29→
    30→namespace LegendreConjecture
    31→
    32→/--
    33→Does there always exist at least one prime between consecutive perfect squares?
    34→-/
    35→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    36→theorem legendre_conjecture :
    37→    answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ n ≥ 1, ∃ p ∈ Set.Ioo (n ^ 2) ((n + 1) ^ 2), Nat.Prime p := by
    38→  sorry
    39→
    40→/-- If there exists a constant `c > 0` such that
    41→`(n + 1).nth Nat.Prime - n.nth Nat.Prime < (n.nth Nat.Prime) ^ (1 / 2 - c)` for all large `n`,
    42→then Legendre's conjecture is asymptotically true. -/
    43→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    44→theorem bounded_gap_legendre
    45→    (H : ∃ c > 0, ∀ᶠ n in atTop, (n + 1).nth Nat.Prime - n.nth Nat.Prime <
    46→      (n.nth Nat.Prime : ℝ) ^ (1 / (2 : ℝ) - c)) :
    47→    ∀ᶠ n in atTop, ∃ p ∈ Set.Ioo (n ^ 2) ((n + 1) ^ 2), Nat.Prime p := by
    48→  sorry
    49→
    50→/--
    51→Ferreira proved that the conjecture is true for sufficiently large n.
    52→-/
    53→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    54→theorem legendre_conjecture.ferreira_large_n :

    56→  sorry
    57→
    58→end LegendreConjecture

    19→/-!
    20→# Oppermann's Conjecture
    21→
    22→*References:*
    23→- [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oppermann%27s_conjecture)
    24→- [Luan Alberto Ferreira, *Real exponential sums over primes and prime gaps*](https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.08725)
    25→-/
    26→
    27→open Finset Filter
    28→
    29→namespace Oppermann
    30→
    31→/--
    32→For every integer $x \ge 2$ there exists a prime between $x(x-1)$ and $x^2$.
    33→-/
    34→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    35→theorem oppermann_conjecture.parts.i (x : ℕ) (hx : 2 ≤ x) :
    36→    ∃ p ∈ Ioo (x * (x - 1)) (x^2), p.Prime := by
    37→  sorry
    38→
    39→/--
    40→For every integer $x \ge 2$ there exists a prime between $x^2$ and $x(x+1)$.
    41→-/
    42→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    43→theorem oppermann_conjecture.parts.ii (x : ℕ) (hx : 2 ≤ x) :
    44→    ∃ p ∈ Ioo (x^2) (x * (x + 1)), p.Prime := by
    45→  sorry
    46→
    47→/--
    48→**Oppermann's Conjecture**:
    49→For every integer $x \ge 2$, the following hold:
    50→- There exists a prime between $x(x-1)$ and $x^2$.
    51→- There exists a prime between $x^2$ and $x(x+1)$.
    52→-/
    53→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    54→theorem oppermann_conjecture (x : ℕ) (hx : 2 ≤ x) :
    55→    (∃ p ∈ Ioo (x * (x - 1)) (x^2), p.Prime) ∧
    56→    (∃ p ∈ Ioo (x^2) (x * (x + 1)), p.Prime) := by
    57→  sorry
    58→
    59→/-- Oppermann's conjecture implies Brocard's conjecture. -/
    60→@[category high_school, AMS 11]
    61→theorem oppermann_implies_brocard (n : ℕ) (hn : 1 ≤ n) (P : type_of% oppermann_conjecture) :
    62→    letI prev := n.nth Nat.Prime
    63→    letI next := (n+1).nth Nat.Prime
    64→    4 ≤ ((Ioo (prev ^ 2) (next ^ 2)).filter Nat.Prime).card := by
    65→  sorry
    66→
    67→/-- Oppermann's conjecture implies Legendre's conjecture. -/
    68→@[category high_school, AMS 11]
    69→theorem oppermann_implies_legendre (n : ℕ) (hn : 1 ≤ n) (P : type_of% oppermann_conjecture) :
    70→    ∃ p ∈ Ioo (n ^ 2) ((n + 1) ^ 2), p.Prime := by
    71→  obtain ⟨⟨p, ph⟩, _⟩ := P (n + 1) (by simpa)
    72→  use p
    73→  refine ⟨Finset.Ioo_subset_Ioo_left ?_ ph.1, ph.2⟩
    74→  push_cast [sq, add_one_mul,le_self_add]
    75→
    76→/--
    77→Ferreira proved that Oppermann's conjecture is true for sufficiently large x.
    78→-/
    79→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    80→theorem oppermann_conjecture.ferreira_large_x : ∀ᶠ x in atTop,
    81→    (∃ p ∈ Ioo (x * (x - 1)) (x^2), p.Prime) ∧
    82→    (∃ p ∈ Ioo (x^2) (x * (x + 1)), p.Prime) := by
    83→  sorry
    84→
    85→end Oppermann

    12→WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    13→See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    14→limitations under the License.
    15→-/
    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 854
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/854](https://www.erdosproblems.com/854)
    23→
    24→Let $n_k$ denote the $k$-th primorial, i.e. the product of the first $k$ primes.
    25→If $1 = a_1 < a_2 < \cdots < a_{\varphi(n_k)} = n_k - 1$ is the sequence of integers
    26→coprime to $n_k$, are there $\gg \max_i (a_{i+1} - a_i)$ many even integers
    27→of the form $a_{j+1} - a_j$?
    28→
    29→This was asked by Erdős in Oberwolfach (most likely in 1986) [Er85c, Ob1].
    30→-/
    31→
    32→open Nat Finset
    33→
    34→namespace Erdos854
    35→
    36→/-- The $k$-th primorial: product of the first $k$ primes.
    37→$\operatorname{primorial}(0) = 1$, $\operatorname{primorial}(1) = 2$,
    38→$\operatorname{primorial}(2) = 6$, $\operatorname{primorial}(3) = 30$. -/
    39→noncomputable def primorial : ℕ → ℕ
    40→  | 0 => 1
    41→  | k + 1 => Nat.nth Nat.Prime k * primorial k
    42→
    43→/-- The sorted list of integers in $\{1, \ldots, n-1\}$ coprime to $n$. -/
    44→noncomputable def coprimeList (n : ℕ) : List ℕ :=
    45→  ((Finset.range n).filter (fun a => 0 < a ∧ Nat.Coprime a n)).sort (· ≤ ·)
    46→
    47→/-- Consecutive differences of a sorted list of natural numbers. -/
    48→def consecutiveDiffs : List ℕ → List ℕ
    49→  | [] => []
    50→  | [_] => []
    51→  | a :: b :: rest => (b - a) :: consecutiveDiffs (b :: rest)
    52→
    53→/-- The set of distinct gap values in the coprime sequence for $n$. -/
    54→noncomputable def gapValues (n : ℕ) : Finset ℕ :=
    55→  (consecutiveDiffs (coprimeList n)).toFinset
    56→
    57→/-- The maximum consecutive gap in the coprime sequence for $n$. -/
    58→noncomputable def maxGap (n : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    59→  (consecutiveDiffs (coprimeList n)).foldl max 0
    60→
    61→/--
    62→Erdős Problem 854 [Er85c, Ob1]:
    63→
    64→Let $n_k$ be the $k$-th primorial. The number of distinct even integers appearing
    65→as consecutive gaps among integers coprime to $n_k$ in $\{1, \ldots, n_k - 1\}$
    66→is $\gg \max_i(a_{i+1} - a_i)$. That is, there exist $C > 0$ and $K_0$ such that for all
    67→$k \geq K_0$, the number of distinct gap values is at least $C$ times the maximum gap.
    68→-/
    69→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    70→theorem erdos_854 : answer(sorry) ↔
    71→    ∃ C : ℝ, C > 0 ∧ ∃ K₀ : ℕ, ∀ k : ℕ, k ≥ K₀ →
    72→      (((gapValues (primorial k)).filter (Even ·)).card : ℝ) ≥
    73→        C * (maxGap (primorial k) : ℝ) := by
    74→  sorry
    75→
    76→end Erdos854
    77→

    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 783
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/783](https://www.erdosproblems.com/783)
    23→
    24→Fix some constant $C > 0$ and let $N$ be large. Let $A \subseteq \{2, \ldots, N\}$ be such that
    25→$(a, b) = 1$ for all $a \neq b \in A$ and $\sum_{n \in A} 1/n \leq C$.
    26→
    27→What choice of such an $A$ minimises the number of integers $m \leq N$ not divisible
    28→by any $a \in A$?
    29→
    30→Erdős suggests the optimal set is consecutive largest primes up to $N$.
    31→Tao conjectures the minimum is $(\rho(e^C) + o(1))N$ where $\rho$ is the Dickman function.
    32→-/
    33→
    34→open Finset BigOperators
    35→
    36→namespace Erdos783
    37→
    38→/-- The Dickman rho function, the unique continuous function $\rho : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$
    39→satisfying $\rho(u) = 1$ for $0 \leq u \leq 1$ and $u \cdot \rho'(u) = -\rho(u-1)$ for
    40→$u > 1$. -/
    41→noncomputable opaque dickmanRho : ℝ → ℝ
    42→
    43→/-- Count of integers in $\{1, \ldots, N\}$ not divisible by any element of $A$. -/
    44→def unsievedCount (N : ℕ) (A : Finset ℕ) : ℕ :=
    45→  ((Finset.range N).image (· + 1)).filter
    46→    (fun m => ∀ a ∈ A, ¬(a ∣ m)) |>.card
    47→
    48→/--
    49→Erdős Problem 783 (Tao's formulation):
    50→
    51→For any $C > 0$, the minimum number of integers in $\{1, \ldots, N\}$ not divisible by
    52→any element of a pairwise coprime set $A \subseteq \{2, \ldots, N\}$ with
    53→$\sum_{a \in A} 1/a \leq C$ is asymptotically $\rho(e^C) \cdot N$, where $\rho$ is the
    54→Dickman function.
    55→
    56→That is, for every $\varepsilon > 0$ and large enough $N$:
    57→(1) There exists a valid $A$ with unsieved count $\leq (\rho(e^C) + \varepsilon) \cdot N$.
    58→(2) Every valid $A$ has unsieved count $\geq (\rho(e^C) - \varepsilon) \cdot N$.
    59→-/
    60→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    61→theorem erdos_783 (C : ℝ) (hC : C > 0) :
    62→    ∀ ε > 0, ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ N ≥ N₀,
    63→      (∃ (A : Finset ℕ),
    64→        (∀ a ∈ A, 2 ≤ a ∧ a ≤ N) ∧
    65→        (∀ a ∈ A, ∀ b ∈ A, a ≠ b → Nat.Coprime a b) ∧
    66→        (∑ a ∈ A, (1 : ℝ) / (a : ℝ) ≤ C) ∧
    67→        (unsievedCount N A : ℝ) ≤ (dickmanRho (Real.exp C) + ε) * ↑N) ∧
    68→      (∀ (A : Finset ℕ),
    69→        (∀ a ∈ A, 2 ≤ a ∧ a ≤ N) →
    70→        (∀ a ∈ A, ∀ b ∈ A, a ≠ b → Nat.Coprime a b) →
    71→        (∑ a ∈ A, (1 : ℝ) / (a : ℝ) ≤ C) →
    72→        (dickmanRho (Real.exp C) - ε) * ↑N ≤ (unsievedCount N A : ℝ)) := by
    73→  sorry
    74→
    75→end Erdos783
    76→

    16→import FormalConjecturesForMathlib.Topology.Algebra.InfiniteSum.Group
    17→import FormalConjecturesForMathlib.Topology.Algebra.InfiniteSum.Order
    18→import Mathlib.Analysis.SpecificLimits.Basic
    19→
    20→/-!
    21→# Sequences with Large Gaps
    22→
    23→*References:*
    24→ - [Wa01] Wang, Yuefei. "On the Fatou set of an entire function with gaps." Tohoku Mathematical
    25→    Journal, Second Series 53.1 (2001): 163-170.
    26→-/
    27→
    28→open Set Filter Topology
    29→open scoped Finset
    30→
    31→/-- A sequence of natural numbers `n₀ < n₁ < ...` is said to have Fabry gaps if `nₖ / k → ∞`.
    32→This is the terminology adopted in [Wa01] and some other sources. -/
    33→def HasFabryGaps (n : ℕ → ℕ) : Prop := StrictMono n ∧ Tendsto (fun k => n k / (k : ℝ)) atTop atTop
    34→
    35→/-- A sequence of natural numbers `n₀ < n₁ < ...` is said to have Fejér gaps if `∑' 1 / nₖ < ∞`.
    36→This is the terminology adopted in [Wa01] and some other sources. -/
    37→def HasFejerGaps (n : ℕ → ℕ) : Prop := StrictMono n ∧ Summable (fun k => (n k : ℝ)⁻¹)
    38→
    39→theorem HasFejerGaps.hasFabryGaps {f : ℕ → ℕ} (hf : HasFejerGaps f) : HasFabryGaps f := by

    23→/-- Say a sequence is lacunary if there exists some $\lambda > 1$ such that

    25→def IsLacunary (n : ℕ → ℕ) : Prop := ∃ c > (1 : ℝ), ∀ᶠ k in atTop, c * n k < n (k + 1)

    31→  refine eventually_atTop.2 ⟨N, fun b hb ↦ ?_⟩
    32→  grw [hc] at h
    33→  simpa using h _ hb
